posts.text:
Morgan Stanley nails it: 

“… Strip away the narrative for a moment and read only the [oil] prices. They describe a market that has weakened across the board…”
posts.summary: Oil prices are declining across the board, indicating broad market weakness rather than localized supply disruptions or demand concerns. Morgan Stanley's analysis suggests the price movements themselves tell a clearer story of overall market deterioration than prevailing narratives about the sector.

posts.text:
Unfortunately for Trump, while obviously connected, crude oil (white line, $/bbl) and gasoline (blue, ¢/gal) are actually different commodities.

Crude market currently loose (yep, temporarily, surprising but true), but gasoline near its tightest ever relative to crude. https://t.co/1pHO9ctTW0
posts.summary: The crude oil market is currently oversupplied while gasoline prices remain unusually high relative to crude oil costs, indicating tight refining capacity or supply constraints in the gasoline market specifically.

posts.text: Another awfully weak trading day in the physical oil windows. Even after sellers offered the largest ever discounts for some WAF crudes, buyers are not emerging. In the North Sea, physical premia for some grades hit a fresh 6-year low.
posts.summary: Physical crude oil markets are experiencing severe weakness, with West African producers offering record discounts and North Sea grades hitting six-year low premiums while buyer demand remains absent. The deteriorating market conditions suggest weakening global oil demand or oversupply pressures in these key trading windows.

posts.text:
Iran has exported 50 million barrels of crude oil since the US-imposed blockade was lifted two weeks ago. This equates to 1.66 million barrels per day for June 2026. Most other countries in the region are still nowhere near pre-war levels.

#OOTT #IranWar #Tankers https://t.co/FJb70n0lbx
posts.summary: Iran has rapidly ramped up crude oil exports to 1.66 million barrels per day following the lifting of a US blockade, while regional competitors remain below pre-war production levels. This supply increase could influence global oil market dynamics and potentially ease supply constraints in the region.

posts.text:
Tanker capacity entering the Gulf (East-to-West Hormuz transits) is running ahead of expectations, at least thus far.

I'm seeing 8 confirmed VLCC entries yesterday alone. 

Trailing 10-day average sitting around ~10 MMbpd.

We'll soon see if this is sustainable, or just an early https://t.co/OpiE2gfjXh
posts.summary: Large tanker flows into the Persian Gulf are exceeding prior forecasts, with a 10-day average around 10 million barrels per day, though sustainability remains uncertain. The higher-than-expected throughput could signal stronger oil demand or supply adjustments, but Johnston indicates this surge may not persist.

posts.text: Gold's rebound above USD 4,000 appears to reflect a combination of short covering after the break below last week's low at USD 3,960 failed to attract sustained follow-through selling, together with some bottom fishing as energy prices continue to ease amid the risk of a https://t.co/RndooCbUyV
posts.summary: Gold's rebound above $4,000 is partly attributed to easing energy prices, suggesting a potential softening in the oil market. The text indicates this price relief may be connected to recession risk, though the specific details are cut off in the incomplete post.

posts.text: Which experts? The debate wasn't whether oil would stay high after the conflict- it was whether the conflict would significantly disrupt supply. Once that risk faded, lower oil prices were a perfectly reasonable expectation. https://t.co/Ne0BgvqZ3x
posts.summary: Patrick De Haan clarifies that the debate about oil prices following a conflict centered on whether supply disruptions would occur, not on prices remaining permanently elevated. Once supply disruption risks diminished, lower oil prices became an expected market outcome rather than an expert failure.

posts.text: Europeans choosing to sweat through 40° C heat  because they believe AC destroys the planet, is hilariously out of date. Refrigerant panic is a generation beyond us. EVEN Texas runs its AC on 30 percent wind and solar.
posts.summary:
# Oil Market Implications Summary

The post argues that environmental concerns about air conditioning are outdated, citing Texas's renewable energy usage as evidence that AC cooling can be powered sustainably. This suggests reduced future oil demand for electricity generation in regions adopting similar renewable-heavy power grids.

posts.text:
India Plans to Add Strategic Fuel Reserves After Iran War Shock

India plans to build strategic reserves of crude, liquefied petroleum gas and liquefied natural gas large enough to meet as much as a month of domestic demand, as the country seeks to avoid a repeat of the supply
posts.summary: India is planning to significantly expand its strategic energy reserves to cover up to one month of domestic demand for crude oil, LPG, and LNG, aiming to insulate itself from supply disruptions. This move signals growing concerns about energy security risks and could increase global demand for storage infrastructure and crude oil purchases.

posts.text: First refining casualty of 2026 heat wave and storms is Marathon refinery in Mandan, ND.  Power outage has temporarily knocked out the 71,000 b/d facility. https://t.co/e6FX5dY2pe
posts.summary: A power outage has temporarily shut down the Marathon refinery in Mandan, North Dakota, removing 71,000 barrels per day of refining capacity from the market during a 2026 heat wave and storms. This represents the first refining facility casualty from the severe weather event.

posts.text: If you think the second mo. of the Iran War led to US gasoline demand destruction, today's EIA report tells a different story. April gas demand measured at 9.123-mbd, up 270,000 b/d from March and the highest April level since 2019. Number is 146,000 b/d above weekly estimates.
posts.summary: U.S. gasoline demand rose to 9.123 million barrels per day in April, the highest level since 2019 and significantly above weekly estimates, contradicting expectations of demand destruction from regional tensions. The increase of 270,000 barrels per day from March suggests resilient fuel consumption despite geopolitical concerns.

posts.text:
Electricity is moving to the centre of Southeast Asia’s energy future, with demand already rising 2x as fast as overall energy use

Electric mobility in particular is growing fast – improving long-term energy security & bringing environmental gains 👉 https://t.co/kv0Ch9TCoO https://t.co/XjfsWCTlDP
posts.summary: Southeast Asia's rapidly growing electricity demand, driven particularly by electric mobility adoption, suggests a structural shift away from oil-dependent transport toward electrified alternatives, with potential implications for regional oil consumption trends. This transition could reduce future oil demand growth in the region as electric vehicles displace traditional petroleum-fueled vehicles.

posts.text:
Global energy demand growth was met by diverse range of sources in 2025 – led by solar & then gas.

While growth slowed from the previous year against a complex economic and geopolitical backdrop, electricity use outpaced overall demand.

More 👉 https://t.co/c9Z4hPfenX https://t.co/nPO3FnCTsf
posts.summary: Global energy demand growth in 2025 was primarily driven by solar and gas rather than oil, suggesting a continued shift away from petroleum. The slowdown in overall demand growth, coupled with electricity use outpacing total energy demand, indicates reduced reliance on oil-based energy sources.

posts.text:
Russia's fuel shortages are rippling across Central Asia. Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan and Uzbekistan are seeing higher fuel prices, while Kazakhstan weighs requests to supply Russia even as it protects its own market. #Russia #Energy #Oil #CentralAsia 

https://t.co/po2PYpWYGS
posts.summary: Russia's fuel shortages are driving up fuel prices across Central Asia, with Kazakhstan facing pressure to supply Russia while managing domestic market needs. The energy disruption highlights regional supply vulnerabilities and competing economic interests among neighboring countries.

posts.text: Oil prices are heading for their biggest quarterly decline since the pandemic as easing Middle East supply fears outweigh geopolitical risk. Markets are now focused on whether U.S.-Iran diplomacy can hold and keep crude flowing. #Oil #OOTT #Brent #WTI #EnergyMarkets
posts.summary: Oil prices are experiencing their largest quarterly decline since the pandemic, driven by reduced concerns over Middle East supply disruptions. The market's direction now hinges on whether U.S.-Iran diplomatic efforts can succeed in maintaining crude supply flows.

posts.text: U.K. PETROLEUM PRODUCT stocks fell sharply in the first two months of the war between the United States and Iran, according to official statistics released for the first time today. By the end of April, gasoline stocks had depleted to the lowest for fourteen years and diesel https://t.co/YJ28mtnwkH
posts.summary: U.K. petroleum product stocks declined significantly during the initial months of a U.S.-Iran conflict, with gasoline inventories reaching their lowest level in 14 years by April. The sharp depletion of fuel reserves suggests potential supply disruptions affecting the oil market.

posts.text: The Revolutionary Guard has told mediators it would close the passage again if it doesn’t get guarantees that Iran is in sole control of the Strait of Hormuz at the Doha talks, say officials familiar with the conversations. It also demands that the U.S. and other countries give https://t.co/ZXeNVXOIuk
posts.summary:
# Summary

Iran's Revolutionary Guard is threatening to close the Strait of Hormuz passage unless it gains sole control of the waterway and receives unspecified guarantees from the U.S. and other countries, potentially disrupting one of the world's critical oil transit routes. The threat raises concerns about global oil supply security, given that a significant portion of the world's petroleum passes through the Strait of Hormuz.

posts.text:
Global vehicle fleet to reach almost three billion by 2050

Future oil demand growth in road transportation comes on the back of a large expansion in the global vehicle fleet, particularly in developing countries. The global vehicle fleet is expected to increase from 1.7 billion https://t.co/yzpsuJLCy4
posts.summary: The global vehicle fleet is projected to nearly double from 1.7 billion to almost 3 billion by 2050, driven primarily by growth in developing countries, which OPEC expects will support future oil demand in the transportation sector.

posts.text:
Ballasters reshape tanker rates

Tanker markets are adjusting to the partial reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. MEG VLCC rates fell as inbound ballasters returned to the region, easing tightness after a period of disruption. In contrast, Atlantic VLCC and Suezmax routes gained https://t.co/s6xOZQN2Je
posts.summary: The partial reopening of the Strait of Hormuz has eased tight tanker rates in the Middle East Gulf region as empty tankers return to load crude, while Atlantic and Suezmax shipping routes have seen rate increases. This rebalancing reflects a shift in oil transport demand away from longer alternative routes back toward the key Persian Gulf chokepoint.

posts.text:

Our 2025-26 energy transition outlook highlighted uneven progress for Middle East decarbonisation – and a US$5.3 trillion investment opportunity between now and 2060.

posts.summary: The Middle East's energy transition will progress unevenly through 2060, requiring $5.3 trillion in investments and potentially reshaping the region's traditional oil-dependent economy. The outlook suggests significant structural changes ahead for Middle Eastern energy markets over the coming decades.
